examples.title: Dynamic Array with Amortized Analysis
examples.description: Demonstrates amortized O(1) time complexity for append operations in a resizable array implementation
examples.explanation: When array is full, we double its capacity. The key insight is that resizing is expensive (O(n)) but happens rarely. Over n operations, the total cost is O(n), giving amortized O(1) per operation. Each element is moved at most once when capacity doubles.

examples.title: Binary Counter with Amortized Increment
examples.description: Implements a binary counter and demonstrates amortized O(1) increment using aggregate analysis
examples.explanation: In a binary counter, the rightmost bit flips on every increment, the second bit flips every 2 operations, the third every 4, etc. Over n increments, the total number of bit flips is at most 2n (specifically n + n/2 + n/4 + ... < 2n). Using aggregate analysis, the amortized cost per increment is O(1).
examples.spaceComplexity: O(b) where b is number of bits

examples.title: Queue Using Two Stacks with Amortized O(1)
examples.description: Implements a queue using two stacks and demonstrates amortized O(1) time complexity using the accounting method
examples.explanation: This implementation uses two stacks: enqueue_stack for incoming elements and dequeue_stack for outgoing elements. When dequeue is called and dequeue_stack is empty, we transfer all elements from enqueue_stack to dequeue_stack (reversing order). Each element is pushed and popped at most twice across n operations, giving amortized O(1) per operation. The accounting method can prove this by assigning cost 2 to enqueue and 0 to dequeue (or vice versa).
